title: Course Structure and Weekly Workflow
text: The AI Engineering course is organized around weekly seminars, homework folders, and short reports. Each seminar introduces one engineering topic and is followed by one homework assignment in the student's repository.

title: Course Structure and Weekly Workflow
text: Each seminar introduces one engineering topic and is followed by one homework assignment in the student's repository.

title: Course Structure and Weekly Workflow
text: Every homework should be placed in homeworks/HWXX with an executable notebook, a report.md file, and the required artifacts folder.

title: Course Structure and Weekly Workflow
text: The recommended workflow is to study the theory, run the demo notebook, adapt the ideas, and then document the final experiment clearly.

title: Submission Rules
text: A homework submission is considered complete only when the repository contains the required files with exact names. The notebook must run from top to bottom without hidden manual steps.

title: Submission Rules
text: The notebook must run from top to bottom without hidden manual steps. Reports should not rename sections from the provided template because the checker expects the same structure.

title: Submission Rules
text: Reports should not rename sections from the provided template because the checker expects the same structure. Artifacts should be generated by the notebook and linked inside report.md.

title: Validation and Test Policy
text: Validation data is used to select a model or configuration, while the test split is reserved for the final check. A common mistake is to repeatedly inspect test quality during development and accidentally overfit to it.

title: Validation and Test Policy
text: A common mistake is to repeatedly inspect test quality during development and accidentally overfit to it. The correct pattern is train on train, choose by validation, and evaluate on test only once at the end.

title: Validation and Test Policy
text: The correct pattern is train on train, choose by validation, and evaluate on test only once at the end. This rule applies to computer vision, NLP, time series, and retrieval experiments.

title: Retrieval Basics
text: Retrieval is different from classification because the goal is to find relevant knowledge fragments instead of predicting one label.

title: Retrieval Basics
text: A retrieval pipeline usually contains chunking, vectorization, indexing, and top-k search. Dense or vector retrieval helps when the wording of the query differs from the wording in the source document.
